city_report.transport: There are no dedicated airport shuttles, however the airport is served as part of the municipal bus network. Use bus No 61 (or N61 at night) for a direct connection to the Central Train Station (Hlavná stanica). Or change for a tram at Trnavské mýto to get to the city (in order to get to the tram stop, use the underpass and the exit marked "Centrum" (city ) and take any tram in the direction towards .

attraction.name: Bratislava Castle
attraction.description: Main castle of the city overlooking the Danube.

attraction.name: St. Martin's Cathedral
attraction.description: Gothic cathedral and former coronation site of kings.

attraction.name: Michael's Gate
attraction.description: The only city gate preserved from the medieval fortifications.

attraction.name: Grassalkovich Palace
attraction.description: The residence of the President of Slovakia.

attraction.name: Slavin Memorial
attraction.description: Monument and cemetery for Soviet soldiers.

attraction.name: UFO Observation Deck
attraction.description: Unique bridge structure with a deck offering city views.

attraction.name: Blue Church
attraction.description: Art Nouveau church famous for its blue color.

attraction.name: Primate's Palace
attraction.description: Neoclassical palace where the Peace of Pressburg was signed.

attraction.name: Old Town Hall
attraction.description: Complex of buildings dating back to the 14th century.

attraction.name: Eurovea
attraction.description: Modern riverside district for shopping and leisure.

city_report.description: Strategically located on the Danube, Bratislava combines competitive operating costs with rapid growth in the service sector. Its human scale allows for high safety standards and a swift transition to green infrastructure.
city_report.wiki_intro: Bratislava (Hungarian: Pozsony, German: Pressburg, known as Prešporok before 1919), is the capital and largest city of Slovakia. It has a population of more than 475,000 (2021), and is the administrative, cultural and economic centre of the country. For most visitors, it is the entry point into the rest of the country. Located on the border tripoint of Slovakia, Austria and Hungary, the city has sat in the very hearts of empires since its earliest mention in the 10th century. As such, the city is a place of contrasts; the city centre features the medieval and renaissance old town, as well as the skyscraper-heavy business and shopping district; the outskirts feature both hillside villas, and communist-era apartment blocks. Bratislava has a very pleasant medieval inner city with narrow, winding streets, a hill-top castle next to the river Danube, and many historic churches and buildings to visit. The old town is centered on two squares, Hlavné námestie (main square) and Hviezdoslavovo námestie (Hviezdoslav square, named after a famous Slovak poet). Of a rather different architectural character are some of the communist-era buildings found in the modern parts of the city; a prime example is Petrzalka housing estate, the biggest Communist-era concrete block housing complex in Central Europe, which stretches on endlessly just across the river. Move further east and there are plenty of rural places to explore. There are farms, vineyards, agricultural land, and tiny villages less...
